quiz.question: She gave unstinting support to the project throughout its most difficult phase. What does 'unstinting' mean?
options: Given freely and without holding back
More than was expected given the circumstances
Maintained even when others had withdrawn their backing
Professional in a way that went beyond personal friendship
quiz.answer: Given freely and without holding back
quiz.explanation:
Unstinting means generous — given without limit or reservation.
• unstinting = giving or given without restraint; generous
• From 'un-' + 'stint' (to be sparing)
• 'Unstinting praise', 'unstinting generosity' — holding nothing back

quiz.question: His vaunting ambition drove him to take risks others would have refused. What does 'vaunting' mean?
options: Boastfully excessive — excessively ambitious or proud
Exceptional in a way that distinguished him from his peers
Strong enough to override any other consideration
Recognized by those who knew him as his defining characteristic
quiz.answer: Boastfully excessive — excessively ambitious or proud
quiz.explanation:
Vaunting means boastfully excessive — showing off with exaggerated pride.
• vaunting = boasting; excessively proud
• From Old French: 'vanter' — to boast
• 'Vaunting ambition' = arrogantly excessive ambition

quiz.question: His doting grandmother sent him gifts every birthday without fail. What does 'doting' mean?
options: Excessively fond and indulgent — showing devoted affection
Generous in a way that reflected how much she missed him
Attentive to his interests in ways his parents did not always manage
Proud enough to want to mark every occasion in his life
quiz.answer: Excessively fond and indulgent — showing devoted affection
quiz.explanation:
Doting means excessively fond — showing devoted, indulgent love.
• doting = excessively fond; showing devoted love
• From 'dote' — to be excessively fond
• 'A doting parent', 'doting grandparent' — love to the point of indulgence

quiz.question: The sting of the criticism was sharpened by the fact that it was accurate. What is a sting?
options: A sharp, painful feeling — the acute hurt of something
A criticism that landed harder because it could not be refuted
The emotional impact of being told an uncomfortable truth
The discomfort caused by recognizing one's own failure
quiz.answer: A sharp, painful feeling — the acute hurt of something
quiz.explanation:
A sting is an acute, painful sensation — physical or emotional.
• sting = a sharp pain; a painful quality
• From Old English: 'stingan' — to pierce
• 'The sting of rejection', 'the sting of a wasp' — sharp, sudden pain

quiz.question: He made everlasting friends during his years at university. What does 'everlasting' mean?
options: Lasting forever or for a very long time
Close enough to survive the distance that followed graduation
More enduring than friendships made in other contexts
The kind of relationship that continued to deepen over time
quiz.answer: Lasting forever or for a very long time
quiz.explanation:
Everlasting means lasting permanently — enduring without end.
• everlasting = lasting forever; enduring
• From 'ever' + 'lasting'
• 'Everlasting love', 'everlasting peace' — never ceasing

quiz.question: He said it jestingly — half joking, half hoping she would laugh. What does 'jesting' mean?
options: In a joking manner — not entirely serious
Carefully enough not to cause offence if she didn't laugh
Cheerfully and without any deeper intention behind the words
With the timing of someone who had said this kind of thing before
quiz.answer: In a joking manner — not entirely serious
quiz.explanation:
Jesting means joking — making or said in jest.
• jesting = joking; not serious
• From Old French: 'geste' — a story, a joke
• 'Jesting tone', 'said in jest' — playful and not entirely serious

quiz.question: The photograph was visually arresting — you couldn't walk past it without stopping. What does 'arresting' mean?
options: Striking and compelling — catching and holding attention
Technically accomplished in ways that experienced viewers noticed
Unusual enough to stand out in a gallery of similar works
Large enough to dominate the wall it was displayed on
quiz.answer: Striking and compelling — catching and holding attention
quiz.explanation:
Arresting means striking — capturing attention and compelling one to stop.
• arresting = striking; attracting and holding attention
• From 'arrest' — to stop
• 'An arresting image', 'an arresting performance' — stopping you in your tracks

quiz.question: The comedian's routine was sidesplitting — the audience barely recovered before the next joke. What does 'sidesplitting' mean?
options: Extremely funny — causing helpless, uncontrolled laughter
Delivered at a pace that made it impossible to catch breath
More consistently funny than anything they had seen before
Full of the kind of humor that worked for everyone present
quiz.answer: Extremely funny — causing helpless, uncontrolled laughter
quiz.explanation:
Sidesplitting means extremely funny — so funny one's sides ache from laughing.
• sidesplitting = causing intense laughter
• From the physical sensation of laughing hard enough to ache at the sides
• 'Sidesplitting comedy', 'sidesplitting routine' — helplessly funny

quiz.question: The earsplitting alarm woke everyone in the building simultaneously. What does 'earsplitting' mean?
options: Extremely loud — painfully and sharply loud
Loud enough to trigger a response from everyone in range
Designed to be impossible to sleep through
So loud that it could be heard throughout the entire structure
quiz.answer: Extremely loud — painfully and sharply loud
quiz.explanation:
Earsplitting means painfully loud — so loud it seems to split the ears.
• earsplitting = extremely loud; painfully so
• From 'ear' + 'split'
• 'An earsplitting shriek', 'earsplitting noise' — loud enough to hurt

quiz.question: The unremitting pressure of the job eventually wore her down. What does 'unremitting' mean?
options: Constant and relentless — never letting up
Heavy enough to affect her health over the long term
Maintained at a level that left no room for rest or recovery
Coming from too many directions to manage effectively
quiz.answer: Constant and relentless — never letting up
quiz.explanation:
Unremitting means relentless — continuing without any pause or reduction.
• unremitting = never relaxing or slackening; constant
• From 'un-' + 'remit' (to relax)
• 'Unremitting effort', 'unremitting pain' — never easing

quiz.question: She was an unwitting participant in the fraud — she had no idea what was happening. What does 'unwitting' mean?
options: Not knowing or aware — doing something without realizing
Too trusting to have questioned what was happening
Involved through a set of circumstances she had not chosen
Deceived in a way that meant she bore no moral responsibility
quiz.answer: Not knowing or aware — doing something without realizing
quiz.explanation:
Unwitting means without knowing — acting without awareness of what is happening.
• unwitting = not aware; unintentional
• From Old English: 'un-' + 'witan' (to know)
• 'Unwitting accomplice', 'unwitting mistake' — done without awareness

quiz.question: She had an intense craving for chocolate during her pregnancy. What is a craving?
options: A powerful desire for something specific
A preference made stronger by physical or hormonal changes
A need strong enough to override normal dietary choices
An appetite for a specific food that could not be ignored
quiz.answer: A powerful desire for something specific
quiz.explanation:
A craving is an intense desire — a powerful, urgent longing for something.
• craving = an intense longing for something
• From Old English: 'crafian' — to demand
• 'A craving for sugar', 'nicotine cravings' — intense, specific desire

quiz.question: A gnawing sense of regret followed her for years after the decision. What does 'gnawing' mean?
options: Persistent and slowly wearing — eating away over time
Present in a way that was hard to ignore or suppress
Intensifying rather than diminishing as the years passed
Produced by a decision she knew had been wrong at the time
quiz.answer: Persistent and slowly wearing — eating away over time
quiz.explanation:
Gnawing means persistent and wearing — slowly consuming like an animal gnawing.
• gnawing = persistently troubling; slowly eating away
• From Old English: 'gnagan' — to gnaw
• 'A gnawing doubt', 'gnawing hunger' — persistent and wearing

quiz.question: The professor wrote a glowing reference that secured him the position. What does 'glowing' mean?
options: Highly enthusiastic and praising
Comprehensive enough to address every concern the employer had
Based on direct observation of his work over several years
Persuasive because it came from someone of high standing
quiz.answer: Highly enthusiastic and praising
quiz.explanation:
Glowing means warmly enthusiastic — full of high praise.
• glowing = warmly appreciative; highly complimentary
• From 'glow' — to emit warmth and light
• 'A glowing report', 'glowing praise' — warm, bright with approval

quiz.question: The survivors gave harrowing accounts of what they had witnessed. What does 'harrowing' mean?
options: Intensely distressing — deeply upsetting to hear or experience
Detailed in a way that made the events feel immediate
Difficult to tell without becoming visibly emotional
So vivid that listeners found them impossible to forget, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Intensely distressing — deeply upsetting to hear or experience
quiz.explanation:
Harrowing means extremely distressing — causing acute suffering or distress.
• harrowing = acutely distressing; agonizing
• From 'harrow' — a spiked farming tool drawn over the soil
• 'A harrowing experience', 'harrowing testimony' — tearing at the emotions

quiz.question: The economy was on an upswing after three years of recession. What is an upswing?
options: An improvement or increase after a period of decline
The beginning of a period of sustained economic growth
A positive trend that followed a difficult three-year period
The kind of growth that gave confidence to investors
quiz.answer: An improvement or increase after a period of decline
quiz.explanation:
An upswing is a rising trend — an improvement after a period of difficulty.
• upswing = an increase; an upward movement in activity or fortune
• From 'up' + 'swing'
• 'An economic upswing', 'on the upswing' — moving upward after a low

quiz.question: The exam was taxing — it required hours of sustained concentration. What does 'taxing' mean?
options: Requiring great effort — mentally or physically demanding
More difficult than candidates had been led to expect, as the evidence showed
Designed to separate the most capable from the rest
Long enough to test not just knowledge but endurance
quiz.answer: Requiring great effort — mentally or physically demanding
quiz.explanation:
Taxing means demanding — making heavy demands on energy or capacity.
• taxing = demanding a lot; burdensome
• From 'tax' — to burden
• 'A taxing problem', 'mentally taxing' — drawing heavily on resources

quiz.question: The underlying cause of the crisis had been ignored for years. What does 'underlying' mean?
options: Fundamental and beneath the surface — the real cause
Identified by experts but not yet addressed by government
More significant than the immediate triggers that got attention
Present for a long time before the crisis brought it into focus
quiz.answer: Fundamental and beneath the surface — the real cause
quiz.explanation:
Underlying means fundamental — the real or deeper factor beneath the surface.
• underlying = fundamental; lying beneath the surface
• From 'under' + 'lying'
• 'Underlying causes', 'underlying assumption' — what is actually beneath

quiz.question: The syrup was so cloying that it made the dessert hard to finish. What does 'cloying' mean?
options: Excessively sweet or rich — sickening through excess
Sweet in a way that did not complement the other flavors
Too dominant a flavor for the balance of the dish
Poorly balanced in a way that overwhelmed the palate
quiz.answer: Excessively sweet or rich — sickening through excess
quiz.explanation:
Cloying means sickeningly sweet — excessive to the point of revulsion.
• cloying = excessively sweet, rich, or sentimental
• From Old French: 'cloyer' — to obstruct
• 'Cloying sentimentality', 'cloying sweetness' — too much of something pleasant

quiz.question: Three candidates were vying for the same position. What does 'vying' mean?
options: Competing eagerly — contending against one another
Preparing different cases for the selection committee
Hoping to be chosen over two equally qualified rivals
Applying through the official process for the role
quiz.answer: Competing eagerly — contending against one another
quiz.explanation:
Vying means competing actively — contending for the same objective.
• vying = competing; contending for superiority
• From Old French: 'vier' — to challenge
• 'Vying for attention', 'vying for position' — active, eager competition

quiz.question: The smell of fresh bread was appetizing even from the street. What does 'appetizing' mean?
options: Stimulating the appetite — making one want to eat
Pleasant enough to attract customers from the surrounding area
Associated so strongly with comfort that it was irresistible
A sign of quality that made the bakery's reputation
quiz.answer: Stimulating the appetite — making one want to eat
quiz.explanation:
Appetizing means stimulating the appetite — making food desirable.
• appetizing = stimulating the appetite; tempting to eat
• From 'appetite' — desire for food
• 'An appetizing meal', 'an appetizing aroma' — making you want to eat

quiz.question: The chef was awarded a Michelin gong for the third year running. What does 'gong' mean here?
options: An award or official honor
The highest recognition available in culinary competitions
A prize given annually by the relevant professional body
The kind of recognition that changed the fortunes of a restaurant
quiz.answer: An award or official honor
quiz.explanation:
A gong (informal) means an award or medal — especially an official honor.
• gong = a metal disk sounded as an instrument; informally, an award
• From Malay: 'gong'
• 'Military gong', 'Michelin gong' — informal British word for a medal or award

quiz.question: The negotiations were prolonged by repeated last-minute demands. What does 'prolong' mean?
options: To make longer — to extend beyond the original duration
To delay through the introduction of additional requirements
To prevent a conclusion from being reached when it was expected
To add more rounds to a process that had been expected to end
quiz.answer: To make longer — to extend beyond the original duration
quiz.explanation:
To prolong means to extend — to make something last longer.
• prolong = to lengthen in time; to extend
• From Latin: 'prolongare' — to lengthen
• 'Prolong a meeting', 'prolong suffering' — extending beyond what was expected

quiz.question: His headstrong refusal to take advice led to a series of avoidable mistakes. What does 'headstrong' mean?
options: Stubbornly determined to do things one's own way
Too confident in his own judgment to listen to others
Unwilling to hear criticism even when it was clearly well-founded
Driven by self-belief to a degree that sometimes proved costly
quiz.answer: Stubbornly determined to do things one's own way
quiz.explanation:
Headstrong means stubbornly self-willed — determined to act one's own way.
• headstrong = stubbornly self-willed; refusing to be directed
• From 'head' + 'strong'
• 'A headstrong child', 'headstrong refusal' — won't be told

quiz.question: The project became bogged down in endless regulatory requirements. What does 'bogged down' mean?
options: Stuck and unable to make progress
Delayed by a process that had not been anticipated
Slowed by requirements that were difficult to navigate
Held back by bureaucracy that others had managed to avoid
quiz.answer: Stuck and unable to make progress
quiz.explanation:
Bogged down means stuck — unable to make progress, like a vehicle in a bog.
• bog = a wet, marshy ground; to become stuck
• From Irish/Gaelic: 'bogach' — soft ground
• 'Bogged down in detail' = unable to move forward

quiz.question: He came in with a hangdog expression after the meeting. What does 'hangdog' mean?
options: Shamefaced and dejected — showing guilt or humiliation
Tired and miserable after an experience he had not expected
Subdued in a way that made clear something had gone wrong
Too embarrassed by what had happened to meet anyone's eyes
quiz.answer: Shamefaced and dejected — showing guilt or humiliation
quiz.explanation:
Hangdog means dejected and shamefaced — like a dog about to be hanged.
• hangdog = dejected; showing guilt or shame
• From the face of a dog guilty of some misdeed
• 'A hangdog expression', 'hangdog look' — guilty and crestfallen

quiz.question: Audiences always root for the underdog. What is an underdog?
options: A person or team expected to lose — at a disadvantage
A competitor whose odds of winning are considered very low
The weaker party in a contest who attracts public sympathy
Any participant who begins from a position of disadvantage
quiz.answer: A person or team expected to lose — at a disadvantage
quiz.explanation:
The underdog is the one expected to lose — at a disadvantage against stronger competition.
• underdog = a person with less power or chance of success
• From dog-fighting: the dog pinned beneath
• 'Underdog story', 'root for the underdog' — sympathy for the weaker party

quiz.question: The children were agog when they heard where they were going for the holiday. What does 'agog' mean?
options: Highly excited and eager to know more
Too surprised to react immediately
Delighted in a way that showed clearly on their faces
Pleased but uncertain whether they had understood correctly
quiz.answer: Highly excited and eager to know more
quiz.explanation:
Agog means eagerly excited — in a state of keen anticipation.
• agog = very eager; excited
• From Old French: 'en gogues' — in a merry mood
• 'All agog', 'agog with excitement' — eagerly and excitedly expecting

quiz.question: The captain maintained a detailed log of the vessel's daily movements. What is a log?
options: An official record of events, especially on a ship
A document required by maritime law
A record maintained by the captain for insurance purposes
A running account of the voyage for use in port
quiz.answer: An official record of events, especially on a ship
quiz.explanation:
A log is an official record — especially the record kept on a ship.
• log = an official record; the speed/distance measurement on ships
• From the piece of wood thrown overboard to measure speed
• 'Ship's log', 'log in', 'logbook' — record keeping

quiz.question: The museum held a catalog of over 20,000 items in its collection. What is a catalog?
options: A complete list of items in a collection or range
A publication listing the artworks held by an institution
A reference document used by staff to locate specific items
A record of acquisitions made since the museum was founded
quiz.answer: A complete list of items in a collection or range
quiz.explanation:
A catalog is a complete list — a systematic record of items in a collection.
• catalog = a complete list; a systematic record
• From Greek: 'katalogos' — a list
• 'Museum catalog', 'catalog of disasters' — a comprehensive list

quiz.question: She had to lug her heavy bags up three flights of stairs. What does 'lug' mean?
options: To carry something heavy with difficulty
To move luggage without assistance through a building
To transport a heavy load without any mechanical help
To drag something up a route that made it particularly difficult
quiz.answer: To carry something heavy with difficulty
quiz.explanation:
To lug means to carry with effort — to haul something heavy.
• lug = to carry with difficulty; to drag
• From Scandinavian: 'lugga' — to pull by the hair
• 'Lug heavy bags', 'lugging equipment' — effortful carrying

quiz.question: After the scandal, she became a social pariah shunned by former friends. What is a pariah?
options: A person rejected and avoided by society — an outcast
Someone whose reputation had been destroyed by a public incident
A person whose behavior had made them impossible to defend
Someone whose former allies had distanced themselves from
quiz.answer: A person rejected and avoided by society — an outcast
quiz.explanation:
A pariah is an outcast — a person excluded from the community.
• pariah = a social outcast; a person generally despised
• From Tamil: 'paraiyar' — a low caste
• 'A social pariah', 'treated as a pariah' — excluded and avoided

quiz.question: The cheetah is the fastest land animal, capable of speeds exceeding 100 km/h. What is a cheetah?
options: A large spotted cat of Africa renowned for its speed
The fastest of the big cats native to the African savanna
A member of the cat family that hunts using its speed
A predator that relies on acceleration rather than endurance
quiz.answer: A large spotted cat of Africa renowned for its speed
quiz.explanation:
A cheetah is a large, fast spotted cat native to Africa and parts of Asia.
• cheetah = Acinonyx jubatus; the world's fastest land animal
• From Hindi: 'cītā' — spotted
• Distinguished from leopards by its slender build and non-retractable claws

quiz.question: The fabric was bleached to remove the stain. What does 'bleach' mean?
options: To whiten or remove color using a chemical agent
To clean a fabric using a chemical cleaning solution
To apply a disinfectant that also removes discoloration
To treat a surface with a substance that kills bacteria
quiz.answer: To whiten or remove color using a chemical agent
quiz.explanation:
To bleach means to whiten — to remove color through a chemical process.
• bleach = to whiten or remove color
• From Old English: 'blæcan' — to whiten
• 'Bleach a shirt', 'bleaching powder' — chemical whitening

quiz.question: The company was sued for breach of contract. What is a breach?
options: A violation or failure to fulfill an obligation
The failure of one party to deliver what was promised
A legal event that triggered the penalty clauses in a contract
An act that gave the injured party grounds to seek compensation
quiz.answer: A violation or failure to fulfill an obligation
quiz.explanation:
A breach is a violation — a failure to fulfill an obligation or duty.
• breach = a violation; a gap or break
• From Old French: 'breche' — a gap
• 'Breach of contract', 'security breach', 'breach the walls' — breaking through

quiz.question: She preached regularly to her congregation about the importance of compassion. What does 'preach' mean?
options: To deliver a religious or moral message to an audience
To use a position of authority to promote ethical values
To share religious teaching in the context of a service
To address a gathering on matters of faith and conduct
quiz.answer: To deliver a religious or moral message to an audience
quiz.explanation:
To preach means to deliver a sermon — to advocate moral or religious beliefs.
• preach = to deliver a sermon; to advocate strongly
• From Latin: 'praedicare' — to proclaim
• 'Preach to the converted', 'preach patience' — advocating values

quiz.question: The government overreached itself by trying to control too many aspects of daily life. What does 'overreach' mean?
options: To go beyond one's capacity or authority — to overextend
To regulate in ways that exceeded what the public would accept
To attempt more than was politically sustainable
To expand power beyond what the mandate allowed
quiz.answer: To go beyond one's capacity or authority — to overextend
quiz.explanation:
To overreach means to go too far — to extend beyond what is sustainable or permitted.
• overreach = to extend too far; to defeat oneself by going too far
• From 'over-' + 'reach'
• 'Overreach your authority', 'overreached and failed' — going too far

quiz.question: The rival company poached three of her best engineers. What does 'poach' mean?
options: To take from another's territory — to lure away unfairly
To offer salaries that were impossible for her to match
To approach her staff directly with offers of employment
To acquire talent by targeting another company's employees
quiz.answer: To take from another's territory — to lure away unfairly
quiz.explanation:
To poach means to take from another's preserve — to encroach unfairly.
• poach = to take game or fish illegally; to take employees from a competitor
• From Old French: 'pochier' — to thrust
• 'Poach staff', 'poach wildlife' — both involve taking from another's preserve

quiz.question: The city's suburbs had gradually encroached on what was once farmland. What does 'encroach' mean?
options: To gradually intrude into or take over another's territory
To expand over many years into adjacent rural areas
To replace farmland through the pressure of urban growth, as the evidence showed
To consume natural space through the demand for housing
quiz.answer: To gradually intrude into or take over another's territory
quiz.explanation:
To encroach means to intrude gradually — to move into another's territory.
• encroach = to gradually intrude on another's territory or rights
• From Old French: 'encrochier' — to seize with a hook
• 'Encroach on rights', 'encroach on land' — creeping intrusion

quiz.question: They took a cautious approach to the negotiations. What is an approach?
options: A way of dealing with something — a method or strategy
A position adopted in preparation for a difficult conversation
The set of principles that guided their behavior at the table
A strategy developed specifically for this kind of situation
quiz.answer: A way of dealing with something — a method or strategy
quiz.explanation:
An approach is a method — a way of handling or dealing with something.
• approach = a way of dealing with something; moving closer
• From Old French: 'approchier' — to come near
• 'Take a different approach', 'our approach' — the strategy adopted

quiz.question: She had learned to detach herself emotionally from cases at work. What does 'detach' mean?
options: To separate from — to remove emotional connection
To use professional training to manage difficult feelings
To develop the distance needed to make good decisions
To protect herself from the emotional weight of her work
quiz.answer: To separate from — to remove emotional connection
quiz.explanation:
To detach means to separate — to disconnect or disengage.
• detach = to separate; to move oneself away emotionally
• From French: 'détacher' — to untie
• 'Detach a component', 'emotionally detached' — separating from connection

quiz.question: He attached great importance to punctuality. What does 'attach' mean?
options: To ascribe or assign — to connect something to something else
To insist on a quality that others did not always share, as the evidence showed
To signal through behavior what he expected of others
To apply a standard consistently to everyone around him
quiz.answer: To ascribe or assign — to connect something to something else
quiz.explanation:
To attach means to connect or ascribe — to associate something with something else.
• attach = to fasten; to ascribe importance or value to
• From Old French: 'atachier' — to fasten
• 'Attach a file', 'attach importance' — connecting or fixing

quiz.question: She beseeched him to reconsider before it was too late. What does 'beseech' mean?
options: To beg urgently and with great emotion
To make a final appeal before the decision became irreversible
To ask with the kind of emotion that was impossible to ignore
To implore someone with everything she had to reconsider
quiz.answer: To beg urgently and with great emotion
quiz.explanation:
To beseech means to beg earnestly — to implore with urgency.
• beseech = to beg earnestly; to implore
• From Old English: 'besecan' — to seek
• 'Beseech you', 'beseeching look' — urgent, emotional appeal

quiz.question: Like the proverbial ostrich, he buried his head and refused to face the problem. What is being described?
options: Refusing to acknowledge or deal with a difficult reality
Avoiding any conversation that might lead to unpleasant conclusions
Hoping that a problem would resolve itself without intervention
Believing that ignorance offered protection from what was happening
quiz.answer: Refusing to acknowledge or deal with a difficult reality
quiz.explanation:
An ostrich (figuratively) refers to someone who ignores danger — avoiding reality.
• ostrich = the large flightless bird; figuratively one who avoids reality
• From the myth that ostriches bury their heads in the sand
• 'Ostrich strategy', 'ostrich mentality' — refusing to face the truth

quiz.question: He had been filching small amounts from the till for months. What does 'filch' mean?
options: To steal small amounts — to pilfer
To take money in a way that avoided immediate detection
To divert small sums from a business over a long period
To steal from a workplace in amounts too small to be noticed
quiz.answer: To steal small amounts — to pilfer
quiz.explanation:
To filch means to steal — especially in small amounts.
• filch = to steal small amounts; to pilfer
• Origin uncertain
• 'Filching from the till', 'filch a pen' — petty theft

quiz.question: The track descended steeply into a dry gulch at the valley bottom. What is a gulch?
options: A deep narrow ravine — especially one formed by a rushing stream
A dried river channel in an arid landscape
A valley too steep-sided to be easily crossed
A gully formed by erosion in dry mountain terrain, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A deep narrow ravine — especially one formed by a rushing stream
quiz.explanation:
A gulch is a deep, narrow ravine — usually formed by a fast-flowing stream.
• gulch = a narrow, steep-sided ravine
• From 'gulp' — to swallow (the stream swallows the path)
• 'A dry gulch', 'a rocky gulch' — western American terrain feature

quiz.question: Pressure was applied to stanch the bleeding. What does 'stanch' mean?
options: To stop the flow of — to stem
To reduce blood loss through the application of pressure
To treat a wound so that recovery could begin
To apply a bandage that would hold the wound closed
quiz.answer: To stop the flow of — to stem
quiz.explanation:
To stanch means to stop a flow — to stem the flow of blood.
• stanch = to stop the flow of blood or another liquid
• From Old French: 'estanchier' — to make watertight
• 'Stanch the bleeding', 'stanch the flow' — stopping a flow

quiz.question: The judge was highly regarded on the bench for his impartiality. What does 'bench' mean here?
options: The seat and role of a judge — the judicial function
The specific court in which he had served for many years
A position that required both intelligence and wisdom
The seniority he had achieved within the judicial system
quiz.answer: The seat and role of a judge — the judicial function
quiz.explanation:
The bench means the judge's seat — and by extension the judiciary itself.
• bench = the seat of a judge; the judiciary
• From 'bench' — the seat on which a judge sits
• 'On the bench', 'raised to the bench' — appointed as a judge

quiz.question: Workers dug a trench to lay the new water pipes. What is a trench?
options: A long, narrow excavation cut into the ground
A channel dug for the installation of underground services
An excavation along the route of a proposed pipeline
A ditch cut to the depth required by engineering specifications
quiz.answer: A long, narrow excavation cut into the ground
quiz.explanation:
A trench is a long narrow cut in the ground — for utilities, drainage, or military defense.
• trench = a long, narrow excavation
• From Old French: 'trenchier' — to cut
• 'Trench warfare', 'dig a trench' — a long narrow cut

quiz.question: The company retrenched, cutting costs and reducing its workforce by 20%. What does 'retrench' mean?
options: To reduce expenditure or scale back operations
To respond to financial pressure by cutting the size of the business
To make redundancies in order to return to profitability
To restructure in ways that reduced both costs and capacity
quiz.answer: To reduce expenditure or scale back operations
quiz.explanation:
To retrench means to reduce — to cut costs or scale back operations.
• retrench = to reduce costs; to cut back
• From French: 'retrancher' — to cut off
• 'Retrench staff', 'corporate retrenchment' — cutting back in response to pressure

quiz.question: The regulations entrenched the advantages of existing market players. What does 'entrench' mean?
options: To establish so firmly that change becomes very difficult
To create regulatory conditions that made competition difficult
To use policy to protect the position of established businesses
To design rules that had the effect of excluding new entrants
quiz.answer: To establish so firmly that change becomes very difficult
quiz.explanation:
To entrench means to establish firmly — so that dislodging becomes very difficult.
• entrench = to establish so firmly as to be difficult to change
• From 'trench' — to dig in
• 'Entrenched interests', 'entrenched positions' — dug in and hard to move

quiz.question: Leaving her hometown was a wrench she had not anticipated. What does 'wrench' mean here?
options: A painful and difficult emotional separation
A more difficult experience than the practical challenges she had expected
A feeling of loss that she had not prepared herself for
An emotional cost that outweighed the professional benefits
quiz.answer: A painful and difficult emotional separation
quiz.explanation:
A wrench means a painful tearing away — an emotionally difficult separation.
• wrench = a sudden violent twist; a painful feeling of parting
• From Old English: 'wrencan' — to twist
• 'A terrible wrench', 'it was a wrench to leave' — painful parting

quiz.question: The cold water quenched her thirst instantly. What does 'quench' mean?
options: To satisfy a thirst or desire completely
To provide immediate relief after a period of deprivation
To cool the body after exertion in warm conditions
To replace lost fluid and restore a normal state
quiz.answer: To satisfy a thirst or desire completely
quiz.explanation:
To quench means to satisfy completely — to extinguish a thirst or fire.
• quench = to satisfy a thirst; to extinguish a fire
• From Old English: 'cwencan' — to extinguish
• 'Quench your thirst', 'quench the flames' — satisfying or extinguishing

quiz.question: In a pinch, she could adapt the recipe using what was in the cupboard. What does 'in a pinch' mean?
options: If absolutely necessary — in an emergency situation
If the alternative was to go without
When no better option was available
In circumstances where improvisation was the only choice
quiz.answer: If absolutely necessary — in an emergency situation
quiz.explanation:
In a pinch means if necessary — when in a tight spot and alternatives are lacking.
• pinch = a tight situation; to squeeze between two surfaces
• From 'pinch' — to squeeze painfully
• 'In a pinch', 'pinch of salt' — either a tight situation or a small amount

quiz.question: The speech lacked punch — it made valid points but failed to land them forcefully. What does 'punch' mean here?
options: Force and impact — the quality of landing effectively
The kind of rhetoric that makes an audience sit up
The intensity needed for key points to be remembered
Rhetorical power that matched the importance of the argument
quiz.answer: Force and impact — the quality of landing effectively
quiz.explanation:
Punch means force — the quality of striking with impact and effectiveness.
• punch = a forceful blow; effective force or impact
• From Middle English: 'punchen' — to stamp
• 'Punch of an argument', 'pack a punch' — effective impact

quiz.question: The politician warned against the mob mentality of trial by social media — effectively lynching people before the facts were established. What does 'lynch' mean?
options: To condemn and punish without a fair legal process
To use social pressure to destroy someone's reputation
To react with collective anger before the evidence was in
To remove a person's livelihood through coordinated online attack
quiz.answer: To condemn and punish without a fair legal process
quiz.explanation:
To lynch means to punish without trial — to condemn and harm without due process.
• lynch = to kill or punish without legal authority by mob action
• Named after Charles Lynch — an 18th-century Virginia judge
• 'Lynch mob', 'social media lynching' — punishment without trial

quiz.question: She fastened her lapel with an antique brooch. What is a brooch?
options: An ornamental pin fastened to clothing
A piece of jewelry worn at the collar or lapel
A decorative clip attached to clothing at the chest
A jeweled fastening used to secure fabric
quiz.answer: An ornamental pin fastened to clothing
quiz.explanation:
A brooch is an ornamental pin — worn on clothing.
• brooch = a decorative pin or clasp worn on clothing
• From Old French: 'broche' — a spit, point
• 'A diamond brooch', 'a vintage brooch' — decorative jewelry fastened to fabric

quiz.question: The invention of the printing press marked the beginning of a new epoch. What is an epoch?
options: A distinctive period in history — a major era
The beginning of a transformation that changed the world
A period defined by a single transformative technology
An era bounded by two events of comparable importance
quiz.answer: A distinctive period in history — a major era
quiz.explanation:
An epoch is a period of history — a significant and distinctive era.
• epoch = a period of time marked by a distinctive character
• From Greek: 'epoche' — a pause, a fixed point
• 'A new epoch', 'epoch-making' — a historically significant period

quiz.question: She gave him an arch look when he claimed he had forgotten. What does 'arch' mean here?
options: Knowing and slyly playful — suggesting amusement at a pretense
Skeptical and clearly not convinced by what he had said, as the evidence showed
Amused in a way that was visible without words
Aware that he was not telling the whole truth
quiz.answer: Knowing and slyly playful — suggesting amusement at a pretense
quiz.explanation:
Arch means slyly playful — knowing and mischievously teasing.
• arch = slyly teasing; knowing
• From 'arch-' — chief (in 'rogue'); the chief characteristic of a rogue
• 'An arch smile', 'arch remark' — knowing and mischievous

quiz.question: The country's oligarchs had acquired their wealth during the post-Soviet privatizations. What is an oligarch?
options: A member of a small group that holds power or wealth
A billionaire who became wealthy through the acquisition of state assets
A powerful figure whose wealth gave him political influence
An individual who benefited from a period of economic transition
quiz.answer: A member of a small group that holds power or wealth
quiz.explanation:
An oligarch is a member of an oligarchy — one of a small group controlling wealth or power.
• oligarch = a member of a small group wielding power; a very wealthy individual
• From Greek: 'oligos' (few) + 'arkhein' (to rule)
• 'Russian oligarchs', 'oligarchy' — rule by the few

quiz.question: The country's monarch had reigned for over sixty years. What is a monarch?
options: A hereditary sovereign — a king, queen, or emperor
The head of state in a country with a royal tradition
The highest-ranking member of a country's ruling family
A ruler whose position is inherited rather than elected
quiz.answer: A hereditary sovereign — a king, queen, or emperor
quiz.explanation:
A monarch is a hereditary sovereign — a king, queen, or emperor.
• monarch = a hereditary head of state
• From Greek: 'mono-' (alone) + 'arkhein' (to rule)
• 'Constitutional monarch', 'absolute monarch' — different types of royal rule

quiz.question: The bakery produced a fresh batch of bread every morning. What is a batch?
options: A quantity of something made or processed at one time
The quantity of bread produced in a single baking session
A production run that maintained consistent quality
The output from a single shift in a bakery
quiz.answer: A quantity of something made or processed at one time
quiz.explanation:
A batch is a quantity produced together — made in one group at one time.
• batch = a quantity produced in one operation
• From Old English: 'bacan' — to bake
• 'Batch of cookies', 'batch processing' — a group made together

quiz.question: The chicks began to hatch on the twentieth day. What does 'hatch' mean?
options: To emerge from an egg — to break out of the shell
To complete the process of incubation and emerge
To develop from a fertilized egg to an independent creature
To be born through the process of breaking an eggshell
quiz.answer: To emerge from an egg — to break out of the shell
quiz.explanation:
To hatch means to emerge from an egg — to break through the shell.
• hatch = to emerge from an egg; to devise a plan
• From Middle English: 'hacchen' — to produce from an egg
• 'Hatch a plan', 'hatch an egg' — bringing something hidden into existence

quiz.question: She latched on to the key point immediately. What does 'latch on' mean?
options: To grasp and understand something quickly
To identify what was important before the speaker finished
To focus on an idea that others had passed over
To connect instantly with a concept that resonated with her
quiz.answer: To grasp and understand something quickly
quiz.explanation:
To latch on means to grasp — to catch on to quickly.
• latch = a fastening; to latch on = to understand; to attach oneself
• From Old English: 'læccan' — to catch
• 'Latch on to an idea', 'latch on to a person' — grasping or attaching

quiz.question: She snatched victory from the jaws of defeat with a last-minute goal. What does 'snatch' mean?
options: To grab quickly — to seize suddenly
To convert a desperate situation into a winning one
To score in circumstances that nobody had expected
To rescue a result that had seemed beyond reach
quiz.answer: To grab quickly — to seize suddenly
quiz.explanation:
To snatch means to grab suddenly — to seize quickly and unexpectedly.
• snatch = to seize quickly; to grab
• From Middle English: 'snacchen' — to snap at
• 'Snatch a win', 'snatch a bag' — sudden seizure

quiz.question: The team worked with great dispatch to meet the deadline. What does 'dispatch' mean?
options: Speed and efficiency — getting things done quickly
The kind of focus that came from working under pressure
The coordinated effort that allowed a complex task to be done
The discipline required to maintain quality under time pressure
quiz.answer: Speed and efficiency — getting things done quickly
quiz.explanation:
Dispatch means speed and efficiency — sending or doing things promptly.
• dispatch = to send off; speed in doing something
• From Spanish: 'despachar' — to expedite
• 'With dispatch', 'dispatch a message' — speed and efficiency

quiz.question: The company ditched the failing product line entirely. What does 'ditch' mean?
options: To abandon completely — to get rid of
To close down a line that had never reached profitability
To redirect resources away from an underperforming product
To accept the loss and focus the business elsewhere
quiz.answer: To abandon completely — to get rid of
quiz.explanation:
To ditch means to abandon — to get rid of something entirely.
• ditch = to throw away; to abandon; also a narrow channel
• From Old English: 'dic' — a ditch
• 'Ditch the plan', 'ditch a friend' — to abandon without ceremony

quiz.question: He was known as a snitch who would tell teachers about anything he saw. What is a snitch?
options: A person who informs on others — a telltale
A student trusted by teachers to report what happened
Someone who used information to improve his own position
A person whose informing made him unpopular with other students
quiz.answer: A person who informs on others — a telltale
quiz.explanation:
A snitch is an informer — someone who tells on others.
• snitch = to inform on someone; a person who informs
• Origin uncertain; possibly from 'snitch' — a fillip on the nose
• 'Don't snitch', 'a school snitch' — a telltale or informer

quiz.question: The sales team delivered a polished pitch to the investors. What is a pitch?
options: A persuasive presentation designed to win support or investment
A formal presentation of a business opportunity
The argument that a team makes to potential backers
A structured case for why an investor should provide funding, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A persuasive presentation designed to win support or investment
quiz.explanation:
A pitch is a persuasive presentation — an attempt to win business or support.
• pitch = a persuasive presentation; also the slope, a sports field, or a musical note
• From 'pitch' — to throw
• 'Sales pitch', 'elevator pitch' — making a compelling case

quiz.question: The rumor was scotched quickly by an official denial. What does 'scotch' mean?
options: To put a decisive end to — to stamp out
To deny publicly before it could spread further
To issue a response that was clear enough to end the matter
To prevent a story from gaining traction in the media
quiz.answer: To put a decisive end to — to stamp out
quiz.explanation:
To scotch means to stop decisively — to put an end to a rumor or plan.
• scotch = to decisively put an end to
• From Old French: 'escocher' — to notch
• 'Scotch a rumor', 'scotch a plan' — putting a definitive end to

quiz.question: She notched up her fifth consecutive win. What does 'notch up' mean?
options: To score or achieve — to add to one's tally
To win in a way that made the achievement feel cumulative
To extend a run that had attracted widespread attention
To add to a record that was already considered impressive
quiz.answer: To score or achieve — to add to one's tally
quiz.explanation:
To notch up means to score — to add to a total.
• notch = a nick or cut used to keep count; to score
• From Old French: 'oche' — a notch
• 'Notch up a win', 'another notch' — scoring another point in a tally

quiz.question: Alcohol had become a crutch for dealing with stress. What is a crutch?
options: A support one leans on — often unhealthily
A way of managing a problem that did not address its cause
A temporary measure that had become a permanent habit
A solution that had made the underlying problem worse over time
quiz.answer: A support one leans on — often unhealthily
quiz.explanation:
A crutch (figuratively) is something leaned on as support — often unhealthily.
• crutch = a support; something relied on too heavily
• From Old English: 'crycc' — a staff
• 'Use as a crutch', 'emotional crutch' — depending too much on something

quiz.question: The troops debouched from the narrow pass into the open plain. What does 'debouch' mean?
options: To emerge from a confined space into a wider area
To complete a march through difficult terrain
To reach a position that offered tactical advantage
To leave a fortified position and advance toward the enemy
quiz.answer: To emerge from a confined space into a wider area
quiz.explanation:
To debouch means to emerge — to come out from a narrow passage into open ground.
• debouch = to emerge from a confined space into open country
• From French: 'déboucher' — to come out of a mouth
• 'Debouch into the plain', 'a river debouches' — opening into wider space

quiz.question: He was a terrible grouch before his morning coffee. What is a grouch?
options: A grumpy, discontented person — a habitual complainer
Someone who needed time before he could be pleasant company
A person whose mood improved dramatically with caffeine
A colleague who was difficult before a certain time of day
quiz.answer: A grumpy, discontented person — a habitual complainer
quiz.explanation:
A grouch is a grumpy person — one who complains and is persistently discontented.
• grouch = a grumpy, complaining person; to grumble
• Origin uncertain; possibly from 'grudge'
• 'An old grouch', 'stop grouching' — habitual grumpiness

quiz.question: The photographer retouched the portrait to remove blemishes. What does 'retouch' mean?
options: To make small improvements to a finished work
To adjust a photograph after it has been taken
To use editing software to improve the appearance of a subject
To correct imperfections in a photograph during post-production
quiz.answer: To make small improvements to a finished work
quiz.explanation:
To retouch means to make minor improvements — to touch up a finished piece.
• retouch = to improve by making small corrections
• From 're-' + 'touch'
• 'Retouch a photograph', 'retouch a painting' — small corrective adjustments

quiz.question: The old craftsman was regarded as a nonesuch in his trade. What is a nonesuch?
options: A person or thing without equal — an unmatched example
The most accomplished practitioner of a dying craft
A figure whose reputation was based on a lifetime of excellence
Someone whose skills set him apart from every other craftsman
quiz.answer: A person or thing without equal — an unmatched example
quiz.explanation:
A nonesuch is something unparalleled — without equal in its kind.
• nonesuch = a person or thing without equal; unmatched
• From 'none such' — none like it
• 'A nonesuch in his field' = without equal

quiz.question: The children rode in a horse-drawn sleigh across the snowy fields. What is a sleigh?
options: A vehicle on runners for traveling over snow
A traditional carriage adapted for winter travel
A horse-drawn transport used before modern vehicles
A vehicle used for recreational travel in snowy landscapes
quiz.answer: A vehicle on runners for traveling over snow
quiz.explanation:
A sleigh is a vehicle on runners — used for traveling over snow and ice.
• sleigh = a vehicle on runners pulled by horses across snow
• From Dutch: 'slee' — a sled
• 'Jingle bells' sleigh; 'sleigh ride' — the classic winter conveyance

quiz.question: The columnist inveighed against the proposed law at every opportunity. What does 'inveigh' mean?
options: To speak or write with forceful opposition against something
To use a platform to oppose a policy one found unacceptable
To argue consistently and publicly against a legislative proposal
To write in strong terms against something one considered wrong
quiz.answer: To speak or write with forceful opposition against something
quiz.explanation:
To inveigh means to rail against — to speak with strong opposition.
• inveigh = to speak or write with strong protest against
• From Latin: 'invehere' — to attack
• 'Inveigh against injustice' — sustained, forceful criticism

quiz.question: The snake sloughed its skin as it grew. What does 'slough' mean?
options: To shed — to cast off an outer layer
To replace the outer surface through a natural process
To lose and replace the skin as part of biological growth
To discard what is no longer needed for the next stage of development
quiz.answer: To shed — to cast off an outer layer
quiz.explanation:
To slough means to shed — to cast off dead skin or an outer layer.
• slough = to shed skin; to cast off; also a swampy hollow
• From Middle English: 'sloh' — a skin
• 'Slough off dead skin', 'slough off bad habits' — shedding what is no longer needed

quiz.question: The museum displayed a holograph letter in the composer's own hand. What is a holograph?
options: A document written entirely in the author's own handwriting
An original manuscript rather than a printed reproduction
A personal letter that provided evidence of a writer's thoughts
A handwritten document of significant historical value
quiz.answer: A document written entirely in the author's own handwriting
quiz.explanation:
A holograph is a document entirely in the author's own hand — not typed or copied.
• holograph = a document written entirely in the author's handwriting
• From Greek: 'holos' (whole) + 'graphein' (to write)
• 'A holograph will', 'a holograph manuscript' — written entirely by hand

quiz.question: The word 'bat' is a homograph — it means both the animal and the sporting implement. What is a homograph?
options: A word spelled the same as another but with a different meaning
A word that shares its sound with another of different meaning, as the evidence showed
A term that has developed multiple meanings over time
A word whose meaning must be inferred from the context
quiz.answer: A word spelled the same as another but with a different meaning
quiz.explanation:
A homograph is a word spelled identically to another but with a different meaning.
• homograph = one of two words spelled alike but different in meaning
• From Greek: 'homos' (same) + 'graphein' (to write)
• 'Bat' (animal), 'bat' (sports) — same spelling, different meaning

quiz.question: She published a monograph on the political thought of the Renaissance. What is a monograph?
options: A detailed scholarly work on a single subject
An academic book examining one thinker's ideas in depth
A published thesis revised for a specialist audience
A research publication based on archival sources
quiz.answer: A detailed scholarly work on a single subject
quiz.explanation:
A monograph is a scholarly work on one topic — a detailed study of a single subject.
• monograph = a detailed scholarly work on a single specific subject
• From Greek: 'mono-' (one) + 'graphein' (to write)
• 'Write a monograph', 'publish a monograph' — focused scholarly study

quiz.question: The epitaph on the gravestone read simply: 'He was kind.' What is an epitaph?
options: An inscription on a tomb or gravestone in memory of the dead
The final tribute paid to a person at their funeral
A statement chosen to capture the essence of a person's life, as the evidence showed
The words selected by a family to mark a loved one's grave
quiz.answer: An inscription on a tomb or gravestone in memory of the dead
quiz.explanation:
An epitaph is a gravestone inscription — words written in memory of the dead.
• epitaph = an inscription on a tomb; a short tribute to the dead
• From Greek: 'epi-' (upon) + 'taphos' (tomb)
• 'A fitting epitaph', 'epitaph for a career' — memorial words

quiz.question: The tomb walls were covered in hieroglyphs that took decades to decipher. What is a hieroglyph?
options: A pictorial symbol used in ancient Egyptian writing
A carved image that represents a word or sound in Egyptian
One of the symbols used in the writing system of ancient Egypt
A pictographic character used in a system of ancient inscription
quiz.answer: A pictorial symbol used in ancient Egyptian writing
quiz.explanation:
A hieroglyph is a pictorial symbol — used in ancient Egyptian writing.
• hieroglyph = a character in Egyptian picture-writing
• From Greek: 'hieros' (sacred) + 'glyphein' (to carve)
• 'Decipher hieroglyphs', 'hieroglyphic writing' — sacred carved symbols

quiz.question: The cave walls were covered in petroglyphs made by ancient inhabitants. What is a petroglyph?
options: An image carved or scratched into rock by prehistoric people
A cave painting from the earliest periods of human habitation
A symbol carved into stone as part of a ritual practice
A prehistoric artwork preserved in natural rock formations
quiz.answer: An image carved or scratched into rock by prehistoric people
quiz.explanation:
A petroglyph is a rock carving — an image pecked or scratched into stone.
• petroglyph = a prehistoric carving or inscription on rock
• From Greek: 'petros' (rock) + 'glyphein' (to carve)
• Distinct from pictographs (painted) — petroglyphs are carved

quiz.question: The slapdash repair job fell apart within a week. What does 'slapdash' mean?
options: Done hastily and carelessly — without proper care or effort
Completed quickly to meet a deadline rather than a standard, as the evidence showed
Poorly planned and inadequately executed
Finished without the attention to detail the job required
quiz.answer: Done hastily and carelessly — without proper care or effort
quiz.explanation:
Slapdash means carelessly rushed — done without proper attention.
• slapdash = done hastily and carelessly
• From 'slap' + 'dash' — splashing on carelessly
• 'A slapdash job', 'slapdash approach' — lacking care

quiz.question: His brash confidence put people off even when he was right. What does 'brash' mean?
options: Boldly self-assertive in an off-putting way — pushy
Confident to a degree that made others feel he was not listening
Right enough often enough to justify his manner
Too sure of himself to show the humility situations called for
quiz.answer: Boldly self-assertive in an off-putting way — pushy
quiz.explanation:
Brash means self-assertive in an off-putting way — loud, pushy, and tactless.
• brash = self-assertive in a rude way; hastily bold
• Origin uncertain
• 'Brash behavior', 'brash confidence' — loud and tactlessly bold

quiz.question: The beauty queen wore a sash bearing the name of her country. What is a sash?
options: A band of fabric worn across the body or around the waist
A decorative strip of material worn as part of formal dress
A ceremonial accessory indicating rank or membership
A ribbon-like item worn at formal or competitive events
quiz.answer: A band of fabric worn across the body or around the waist
quiz.explanation:
A sash is a band of fabric — worn across the body, around the waist, or over the shoulder.
• sash = a band of fabric worn for decoration or as a badge
• From Arabic: 'shash' — fine muslin
• 'A presidential sash', 'a window sash' — a decorative band

quiz.question: He had a stash of emergency cash hidden under the floorboards. What is a stash?
options: A secret store or hoard of something
A quantity of something hidden for future use
A supply kept where only the owner knew to find it
A reserve maintained against an uncertain future need
quiz.answer: A secret store or hoard of something
quiz.explanation:
A stash is a hidden store — a secret supply of something kept aside.
• stash = a hidden store; to hide away secretly
• Origin uncertain
• 'A stash of cash', 'stash drugs' — a hidden supply

quiz.question: The team's working styles meshed well together. What does 'mesh' mean?
options: To fit together harmoniously — to interlock and work together
To produce enough common ground to allow collaboration
To complement each other in a way that improved the result, as the evidence showed
To work alongside each other without creating friction
quiz.answer: To fit together harmoniously — to interlock and work together
quiz.explanation:
To mesh means to fit together — to interlock and work in harmony.
• mesh = to interlock; to work together harmoniously
• From Old French: 'maille' — a link
• 'Mesh gears', 'ideas mesh well' — fitting together without friction

quiz.question: She became enmeshed in a web of obligations she could not escape. What does 'enmesh' mean?
options: To entangle — to catch in a mesh of complications
To accept commitments that would later prove hard to honor
To become involved in a way that made withdrawal impossible
To allow circumstances to constrain her freedom of action
quiz.answer: To entangle — to catch in a mesh of complications
quiz.explanation:
To enmesh means to entangle — to catch up in a complicated network.
• enmesh = to cause to become entangled
• From 'en-' + 'mesh'
• 'Enmeshed in debt', 'enmeshed in a plot' — caught and unable to free oneself

quiz.question: The grain was threshed to separate the kernels from the stalks. What does 'thresh' mean?
options: To separate grain from husks by beating
To process grain using mechanical or manual methods
To beat harvested crops to release the edible seeds
To prepare grain for milling by removing the unwanted parts
quiz.answer: To separate grain from husks by beating
quiz.explanation:
To thresh means to separate grain — to beat harvested crops to remove the grain.
• thresh = to separate grain from stalks and husks by beating
• From Old English: 'threscan'
• 'Thresh wheat', 'threshing machine' — separating grain from straw

quiz.question: She furbished the old desk until it looked nearly new. What does 'furbish' mean?
options: To polish and restore — to clean and brighten
To sand and lacquer a wooden surface until it shone
To treat an old piece so it could be used again
To restore the appearance of something through careful work
quiz.answer: To polish and restore — to clean and brighten
quiz.explanation:
To furbish means to polish — to clean, restore, and brighten up.
• furbish = to restore by cleaning and polishing
• From Old French: 'fourbir' — to polish
• 'Furbish up', 'furbished armor' — polishing and restoring

quiz.question: The hotel was refurbished at a cost of £5 million. What does 'refurbish' mean?
options: To renovate and restore to a good condition
To invest in the physical upgrade of an existing property
To bring a building up to the standard expected by modern guests
To replace or repair the fixtures and fittings of a property
quiz.answer: To renovate and restore to a good condition
quiz.explanation:
To refurbish means to renovate — to restore to good condition through upgrade.
• refurbish = to renovate and redecorate
• From 're-' + 'furbish'
• 'Refurbish an office', 'refurbished building' — restored to good condition

quiz.question: The diet was dismissed as faddish by most nutritionists. What does 'faddish' mean?
options: Following a passing trend — based on a fad rather than evidence
Popular only among those who prioritized novelty over nutrition, as the evidence showed
Likely to be forgotten once the next trend arrived
Promoted by enthusiasts but lacking scientific support
quiz.answer: Following a passing trend — based on a fad rather than evidence
quiz.explanation:
Faddish means based on a passing craze — following fashionable but temporary trends.
• faddish = based on a fad; following a passing trend
• From 'fad' — a craze
• 'Faddish diet', 'faddish approach' — trendy but not lasting

quiz.question: His outlandish outfit attracted stares wherever he went. What does 'outlandish' mean?
options: Bizarre and strikingly strange — far from the ordinary
Bold enough to attract attention without being fashionable
Distinctive to the point where it became his personal brand
Unusual by any standard but worn with complete confidence
quiz.answer: Bizarre and strikingly strange — far from the ordinary
quiz.explanation:
Outlandish means strikingly bizarre — far from normal conventions.
• outlandish = bizarre; strikingly strange or foreign
• From Old English: 'utland' — foreign land
• 'Outlandish costume', 'outlandish claim' — beyond the pale of the normal

quiz.question: The café had a modish interior that appealed to a young urban crowd. What does 'modish' mean?
options: Fashionably current — following the latest trends
Designed to appeal to customers with specific aesthetic tastes
Decorated in a way that suggested awareness of current trends
Attractive to a demographic that valued design and style
quiz.answer: Fashionably current — following the latest trends
quiz.explanation:
Modish means fashionable — following current fashion or style.
• modish = conforming to fashion; fashionable
• From 'mode' — fashion
• 'Modish design', 'modish accessories' — currently fashionable

quiz.question: Her prudish reaction to the mildly risqué joke made everyone uncomfortable. What does 'prudish' mean?
options: Excessively proper and easily shocked by indelicacy
Too quick to take offence at humor others found harmless
Unwilling to engage with anything that touched on adult themes
Inclined to disapprove of others in a self-righteous way
quiz.answer: Excessively proper and easily shocked by indelicacy
quiz.explanation:
Prudish means excessively proper — easily offended by anything indelicate.
• prudish = having or showing excessive propriety; easily shocked
• From 'prude' — one who is excessively proper
• 'Prudish attitude', 'prudish response' — taking offence at mild indelicacy

quiz.question: He had a raffish charm that made him simultaneously attractive and untrustworthy. What does 'raffish' mean?
options: Unconventionally dashing — attractively disreputable
Charming in a way that was inseparable from his unreliability
Good-looking enough to make people overlook his character
Stylish in a way that suggested a life lived outside the rules
quiz.answer: Unconventionally dashing — attractively disreputable
quiz.explanation:
Raffish means attractively disreputable — dashing but with a dubious edge.
• raffish = having a slightly disreputable but attractive quality
• From 'raff' — rabble
• 'A raffish charm', 'raffish appearance' — attractive but suggests impropriety

quiz.question: She seemed offish at first, but warmed up once she got to know them. What does 'offish' mean?
options: Distant and aloof — not inclined to be friendly
Reserved in a way that could be mistaken for unfriendliness
Cold initially but capable of warmth once trust was established
Uncomfortable in social situations she had not chosen
quiz.answer: Distant and aloof — not inclined to be friendly
quiz.explanation:
Offish means aloof — distant and not inclined to be sociable.
• offish = distant; not friendly; aloof
• From 'off' — standing apart
• 'An offish manner', 'seemed offish at first' — keeping one's distance
